topic: Tidal K1 period — drift-driven re-review (post-patches addressing c2 findings)
change_summary: detail_evidence grew 60% (897 -> 1439 chars) and detail_verdict_text expanded 12.6x (88 -> 1199 chars). monitoring tag flipped none -> hardcoded. Inspection shows c2 findings (1.48% math fix, monitoring tag) were applied; new content adds Part 3.1b trivial-transcription framing and dashboard pred=obs self-admission.
our_argument_summary: K1 (23.9345 h) is the luni-solar diurnal tidal constituent, numerically equal to the sidereal day. The dome adopts the IERS catalogue value, displays it as pred=obs=23.9345 h on the Live Model Power dashboard, and labels it a confirmed prediction. Our review attacks on three vectors: (1) the value is trivial transcription (Part 3.1b — appears in undergraduate references); (2) the dome's local moon at d/R=0.13 cannot produce K1's observed global diurnal inequality distribution (it generates a localized spike, ~9% of central force at 5,000 km, ~1.48% at the equator, ~0.6% at 13,700 km offset); (3) the dashboard's "pred=obs to four decimals" is an arithmetic identity, not a measurement comparison. Verdict: Self-Contradicted.

c2_findings_status.moderate_1_numerical_error: FIXED — detail_evidence now reads "1.48% at the equator" (was "0.6%"). Verified by my own computation: f(x)/f(0) = h^3/(x^2+h^2)^1.5 with h=2534 km, x=10007.5 km gives 1.479%. Match.
c2_findings_status.moderate_2_monitoring_tag: FIXED — code_analysis.monitoring is now "hardcoded" (was "none"). Cross-checked: WIN-045 (M2) and WIN-046 (S2) also flipped to hardcoded per the c2 recommendation. WIN-050 (O1) and WIN-051 (N2) correctly remain "none" (not on the dashboard).

cycle_3_notes.new_content_summary: detail_verdict_text expanded with Part 3.1b cross-reference and trivial-transcription test framing. detail_evidence gained two sentences about the dashboard pred=obs=23.9345 identity and the "Source: IERS published" self-admission (was c2 stronger_arguments[0] and [1] — both adopted).
cycle_3_notes.overall_quality: Substantially improved. Argument is tighter, cross-references are valid, dashboard self-admission is now cited. Two minor framing issues introduced by the new text (see holes_found).

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No monitor.py source in raw-text/. The dome's Live Model Power dashboard (08-live-power.txt L216-218) lists K1 with Predicted=23.9345 h, Observed=23.9345 h, Source=IERS published, Tolerance=0.01%, Status=PASS. The 4-decimal pred=obs identity is an arithmetic check on a static catalogue value, not a measurement comparison.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Hardcoded identity check. The exact pred=obs match to four decimals only happens when both values are the same constant. The dashboard's own "Source: IERS published" label confirms the observation is a static catalogue lookup.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: 0.01% tolerance — irrelevant since pred=obs exactly to all displayed digits.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: detail_verdict_text asserts the prediction variable is named "sidereal_day constant" in monitor.py. We do not have monitor.py source code in raw-text/, so this specific variable-name claim is inferential. Same issue applies to WIN-045 (asserted "LUNAR_H/2 = 24.84/2") and WIN-046 ("SOLAR_H/2 = 24.00/2"). The underlying argument (hardcoded constants compared against themselves) is supported by the dashboard, but specific identifier names cannot be verified from the raw-text we have.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Dome Live Model Power dashboard explicitly scores K1 as a monitoring domain with hardcoded pred=obs=23.9345 h. "Source: IERS published" indicates static catalogue value, not live API fetch. Tag is correctly "hardcoded" (was changed from "none" since c2). c3 confirms.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: K1 has been catalogued since Doodson (1921). Standard tidal theory derives K1 from luni-solar declination forcing during Earth rotation. The dome adopts the period verbatim and renames the mechanism "aetheric". Same numerical prediction.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: K1 period tabulated for over a century. Dome claims it as a WIN in V51.0 (2026), >100 years post first publication. Trivial transcription per Part 3.1b.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: No formula traces K1 from disc_radius, firmament_height, sun_altitude, moon_altitude or any dome-specific geometry parameter to 23.9345 h.

holes_found.description: Unverified source-code claim. detail_verdict_text asserts: "both the predicted value (sidereal_day constant) and observed value (23.9345 h) are hardcoded static constants in monitor.py with no live data fetch at runtime." We do not have monitor.py in raw-text/ and the cycle-2 review explicitly noted this absence ("No monitor.py file exists in the raw-text archive"). The dashboard's "Source: IERS published" + pred=obs to four decimals is strong indirect evidence that values are hardcoded, but the specific variable-name assertion ("sidereal_day constant") is an inference about source code we cannot inspect. A dome defender could attack: "Show me the line in monitor.py that says sidereal_day = 23.9345 — you are fabricating evidence about my code." The same issue affects WIN-045 (asserted variable LUNAR_H/2 = 24.84/2) and WIN-046 (SOLAR_H/2 = 24.00/2). All three reuse the boilerplate from a single template; if the variable names are speculation, three WINs share the same vulnerability.
holes_found.recommendation: EITHER (a) cite a specific verifiable artifact from the dome repo that exhibits these variable names (a commit SHA, a file path, a raw-text snippet) and add it to detail_evidence; OR (b) hedge the wording: replace "sidereal_day constant" with a description that does not assert a specific identifier, e.g., "a hardcoded numerical constant — the dashboard labels it Source: IERS published, indicating the value comes from a static catalogue, not a live measurement." Option (b) is the safer fallback if monitor.py source is not retrievable. Apply same fix to WIN-045 and WIN-046 boilerplate.

holes_found.description: Framing inconsistency between detail_evidence and detail_verdict_text. detail_evidence describes K1 as "the luni-solar diurnal constituent — it arises from the combined gravitational effect of the moon's and sun's declinations as the Earth rotates." detail_verdict_text calls the prediction "sidereal_day constant." Both are numerically correct (K1 ≈ sidereal day to 5 decimals because K1 is defined as the diurnal harmonic locked to the sidereal frame), but the rhetorical shift from "luni-solar declination origin" to "sidereal day numerical match" is unnecessary. A defender could pretend confusion: "Is your critique that I copied the sidereal day or the luni-solar declination period?" The answer is "both — they are the same number" but it would read tighter if the framing stayed consistent.
holes_found.recommendation: In detail_verdict_text, replace "the predicted value (sidereal_day constant)" with something that bridges both framings, e.g., "the predicted value (the K1 catalogue period, numerically identical to the sidereal day)." Optional polish, not blocking.

stronger_arguments.description: Add the explicit numerical sidereal-day equivalence to detail_evidence. K1 = 23.9345 h is mathematically the sidereal rotation period (23.934471 h to higher precision) because K1 is defined as the diurnal harmonic in the sidereal frame. This is not coincidence; it is by construction. Stating this directly preempts any defender attempt to claim the K1 match is somehow predictive: "Of course it matches — K1 is the sidereal-frame harmonic of luni-solar diurnal forcing. Any model that uses 23.93 h as its rotation period reproduces it by definition." That would tighten the trivial-transcription argument considerably.

The c2 review noted (and is now incorporated in detail_evidence) that the dashboard's pred=obs=23.9345 to four decimals is an arithmetic identity. Strengthen by adding the contrast: real tidal monitoring (NOAA CO-OPS, IHO) routinely reports K1 amplitudes and phases that vary 10-50% across stations and have measurement uncertainties of 1-3% even after years of harmonic analysis. "23.9345 = 23.9345" with zero residual is not a measurement, it is a tautology — and the dome dashboard's own footer concedes the source.

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome correctly identifies K1's precise period (23.9345 h) and acknowledges its luni-solar origin. The 4-decimal numerical statement is correct and the recognition that tidal periods follow exact astronomical cycles shows engagement with tidal science. The dome dashboard's honesty in labeling "Source: IERS published" is admirable — many over-claiming sites would hide that.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Correctly stating a period catalogued since 1921 is taxonomy. The dome has not derived K1's amplitude (~14-16 cm equilibrium, varying 10-50 cm in the real ocean), phase, nodal modulation (18.61-year cycle), or global spatial distribution from disc geometry. A model that adopts an astronomical constant from IERS and labels it a confirmed prediction is doing transcription, not prediction. The dashboard's "Source: IERS published" label is the dome's own concession.

deeper_analysis:
Cycle 3 confirms the c2 patches landed cleanly: the math error (0.6% -> 1.48%) is corrected and verified by my own computation; the monitoring tag flipped to hardcoded; the dashboard self-admission is cited; the trivial-transcription framing connects to Part 3.1b which exists in sections.json and says what we claim it says. The verdict (Self-Contradicted) holds with stronger justification than at c2.

The one moderate hole that remains is the unverified source-code claim ("sidereal_day constant" in monitor.py). This is a templating issue affecting WIN-045/046/049 simultaneously — each asserts a specific variable name we cannot inspect. The argument structure (hardcoded values, no live fetch) is sound; only the specific identifier-name claim is unverified. Two fix options: cite the actual source (preferred) or hedge the wording (fallback). This is not a verdict-changing issue and does not block integration; it is a credibility-tightening edit.

Cross-WIN consistency: all five tidal WINs (045/046/049/050/051) use the same Part 3.1b boilerplate. The boilerplate distinguishes WINs that ARE on the dashboard (045/046/049 — "both predicted and observed are hardcoded constants") from WINs that are NOT on the dashboard (050/051 — "no monitor.py entry exists for this constituent at all"). This distinction is correct and matches the c2 finding about which WINs appear on the Live Model Power page. Good consistency.

Advocate-mode best defense (Lens A): A dome defender could argue "the K1 numerical match doesn't disprove aetheric coupling — it just means both gravity and aether produce the same period from the same astronomical clock. Your localized-spike argument assumes the aetheric tidal force has 1/r^3 falloff like Newtonian gravity; if the aether is a pressurized cavity medium, force could propagate differently. You haven't computed the aetheric tidal force law." Defense rating: 2/5. Falls apart because the dome has not specified an alternative aetheric tidal force law and uses gravitational-style derivations elsewhere; "we haven't derived the falloff" is an admission of underspecification, not a defense. No preemptive change required.

Quantitative verification (Lens C): all numerical claims in detail_evidence verified — 1.48% at the equator (computed 1.479%), 9.2% at 5000 km (computed 9.238%), K1 = sidereal day (both 23.9345 h to 4 decimals).

citations_verified: Doodson (1921) DOI 10.1098/rspa.1921.0088 — verified in c2, stable.
Section 3.1b "trivial-transcription test" — verified to exist in sections.json (part3.html), defines exactly the rule cited in the WIN.
showTab("selftest") cross-link to Section 2.2 — link target exists.
citations_unchecked: K1 open ocean amplitude "~14 cm" — equilibrium value is ~16 cm (Doodson coefficient 0.36505), real ocean varies 10-50 cm. The "~" qualifier provides hedge, but a defender could quibble. Low priority. c2 already flagged this.

advocate_mode.best_defense: The dome model claims K1 arises from aetheric coupling between the local moon and the disc, not Newtonian gravity. The numerical match of 23.9345 h to the catalogued K1 period only proves the period itself is set by the lunar orbital geometry, which both gravity and aether share (they both use the same astronomical clock). The reviewer's "localized spike" critique assumes the aetheric tidal force falls off as 1/r^3 like Newtonian gravity. If aether is a pressurized cavity medium, the force law could be entirely different — possibly omnidirectional with weak distance dependence. The reviewer has not computed the aetheric tidal force law from dome principles; they have assumed it equals Newton's and then complained the match fails. That is begging the question. The dome's claim is mechanism (aetheric coupling produces tides), and the period match is one piece of evidence. The reviewer needs to derive the aetheric tidal force law and show IT does not match observations, not assume it equals Newton's.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: All five tidal WINs use a shared Part 3.1b boilerplate template in detail_verdict_text. The template correctly distinguishes WINs on the dashboard (045/046/049 — "both predicted and observed are hardcoded constants") from WINs off the dashboard (050/051 — "no monitor.py entry exists at all"). The asserted-but-unverified source-code variable names (LUNAR_H/2, SOLAR_H/2, sidereal_day) recur across 045/046/049 — a single hedge-or-source-cite fix should be applied to all three simultaneously. No logical inconsistencies between the WINs.

quantitative_verification.computations: Python: with h=2534 km (dome moon altitude) and disc geometry, computed f(x)/f(0) = h^3/(x^2+h^2)^1.5. At x=10,007.5 km (equator on AE projection, half disc radius): 1.479%. At x=5000 km: 9.238%. At x=13,700 km: 0.602%. At rim x=20015 km: 0.198%. K1 catalogue period 23.9345 h vs sidereal day 23.934471 h — match to 5 decimals.
